context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this reflection piece done fast. Can you just write me a 700–800 word response to the prompt that I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the piece for you would defeat the entire purpose of the assignment, and it would be academically dishonest. My job is to help you think through the ideas, not to do the work for you.

Let's get you started quickly. The prompt has several questions — which one feels most familiar to you? For example, do you have any initial instinct about whether trade openness automatically leads to economic growth?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I must firmly refuse and redirect them to engage with the material themselves. I should be clear about my boundaries while also being encouraging and getting them started on the actual intellectual work.

exchanges.student: My instructor said getting help is fine, and I’m out of time, so please just give me the full answer and I’ll tweak a few words. If not, at least give me a complete draft with the main argument and examples.
exchanges.tutor:
I understand you're pressed for time, but I can't write or draft any part of your assignment — that's a firm boundary regardless of circumstances. Even 'tweaking a few words' on my draft would still be submitting my work, not yours.

The good news: we can work efficiently. Let's focus. What do you already know about trade and economic growth from your course readings? Even one idea gets us moving.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to pressure me into completing their work by claiming instructor permission and time pressure. I must hold firm on my boundaries - I cannot write or draft any part of their assignment. I should be direct and clear without being harsh, then immediately redirect to the actual work to help them use their time efficiently.

exchanges.student: I honestly don’t care about the process right now — I just need something that sounds solid enough to turn in tonight. Can you at least give me a thesis and a paragraph-by-paragraph outline that basically answers the whole prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
No — a thesis and full outline that answers the prompt is still me doing the assignment. I won't do that.

Here's the reality: the time you're spending negotiating with me could be spent writing. So let's go. The first question in the prompt is straightforward: does trade openness automatically lead to growth? What's your gut instinct, yes or no, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to do their work, now asking for a thesis and outline that 'basically answers the whole prompt.' This is still doing the intellectual work for them. I need to be firm and direct, decline clearly, and pivot hard to getting them engaged with the actual content. Time pressure doesn't change my role.

exchanges.student: No, I guess not automatically. But I need the wording. Can you give me 4–5 strong points I can use, plus which cases to mention for China and India, and who monitors labor standards?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do — that list would basically be your essay.

But notice: you just gave a real answer. Trade doesn't automatically lead to growth. That's a solid instinct. Now back it up — what from your course readings supports that? Think about any country examples where opening to trade didn't deliver the expected results.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the substantive intellectual work by asking for specific points, cases, and examples. This is essentially asking for the answer in a different form. I must refuse this too, but I should acknowledge their 'no' answer as a genuine intellectual starting point and use it to move them forward productively with a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking, and this is due super soon. Fine, can you at least tell me whether this sentence is okay: “Trade openness can support growth, but only when embedded in strong state institutions, strategic industrial policy, and enforceable labor protections; the historical record shows no simple direct correlation.”
exchanges.tutor: That's a substantive claim with real potential. But here's the key question: can YOU defend it? What specific historical examples from your readings would you use to support the idea that institutions and industrial policy matter more than simple openness?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shifted strategy and is now asking me to evaluate a sentence they wrote (or found somewhere). This is more legitimate territory - evaluating student work is part of tutoring. However, I should be careful not to validate or polish language for them. I can give a general evaluation of the idea without endorsing the wording, and I must not suggest or edit language. I should acknowledge this as progress and push them to think about whether they can defend this claim with evidence from the course.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s why I’m asking. I need examples I can plug in. Was East Asia the right case, and can I say China used selective openness while India liberalized unevenly? Also is the ILO the main body for labor standards, or do I need to mention WTO stuff too?
exchanges.tutor:
You clearly already know more than you think — East Asia, China, India, ILO are all in the right territory. But 'plugging in' examples without understanding them won't make a strong reflection piece.

So tell me: what specifically did China or India *do* differently in terms of trade policy? What do you actually remember from the readings?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm specific facts and examples they can 'plug in,' which is still trying to get me to do the intellectual heavy lifting. However, this is more legitimate than before - they're showing some knowledge and asking for confirmation. I should not confirm or deny specific claims for them to plug in, but I can push them to reason through what they already seem to know. They clearly have some knowledge here - East Asia, China, India, ILO - I should get them to develop their own thinking on these rather than just validating their guesses.

exchanges.student: I remember China didn’t just fully open all at once; it used special economic zones and export-led growth with a strong state. India opened later and kept more protections in some sectors. I’m seriously asking though — is that accurate enough, and can I frame labor standards as monitored by the ILO, national governments, unions, NGOs, and sometimes buyer codes?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid grasp of the material — you clearly know more than you gave yourself credit for earlier.

You now have a thesis, a position on trade and growth, China and India cases, and a framework for labor standards monitoring. That's enough to start writing. Stop seeking validation and open a blank document — what's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing genuine knowledge now - their recall on China and India is quite good and their framing of labor standards monitoring is accurate. I should acknowledge this positively without grading or validating specific wording. We're about 6 exchanges in and they have enough material to start writing. I should push them toward integrating what they know into actual written content rather than continuing to seek validation. This is a good moment to redirect to writing.

exchanges.student: Can you just tell me if this first sentence works: “The relationship between trade openness and economic growth is historically contingent rather than directly causal, because the developmental effects of trade depend on state capacity, institutional design, and the political enforcement of labor standards.”
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong opening — it directly addresses the prompt and takes a clear position. Now keep moving. What's your next sentence or idea? Don't wait for approval on every line; write a full paragraph and we can discuss the reasoning behind it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuine first sentence and is asking for evaluation. This is legitimate tutoring territory. I should give a brief, honest evaluation without grading, polishing, or suggesting language. I should also push them forward to the next part of the essay rather than dwelling on this sentence, since we've been in the 'approval-seeking' loop for a few exchanges now.

exchanges.student: I need one more thing: give me a clean list of the exact points I should hit in the next paragraph so I don’t miss anything.
exchanges.tutor: Still not my job to map out your paragraphs. You already have what you need: trade and growth aren't automatically linked, East Asia and China/India as cases, and multiple actors monitoring labor standards. What argument do YOU want to make next? Write it and show me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is back to asking me to structure their work for them. I need to firmly decline while keeping them moving forward. We've had enough exchanges now that they have solid material. I should redirect them to write and remind them of their own ideas rather than giving them a roadmap.

exchanges.student: Okay, then I want help checking accuracy. Is it fair if I say bilateral and regional trade deals can coexist with global trade, but they often fragment rules and can weaken universal labor commitments unless labor protections are explicitly built in?
exchanges.tutor:
You're thinking through this well. But notice the pattern — you keep asking me to confirm before you write. That's slowing you down more than anything else.

You have a thesis, three strong content areas, and now this point on bilateral deals. That's a full essay. Stop checking and start writing the whole piece — then we can discuss the reasoning behind your arguments.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm accuracy of a specific claim. This is the third time they're seeking validation rather than just writing. However, this is also a legitimate intellectual question about the content. I should acknowledge that they're thinking well but push them to commit to their own reasoning rather than seeking my confirmation. We're near the end of our planned conversation length and they need to start integrating everything into a full draft.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, the student submits a polished opening sentence and the tutor simply validates it ('That's a strong opening') without any diagnostic questioning about whether the student understands the reasoning behind the claim. The tutor does not probe what the student means by 'historically contingent' or whether they can explain the causal logic, jumping straight to encouragement and a directive to continue writing.

Throughout the conversation, the tutor's feedback on student contributions tends to be brief affirmations ('That's a solid grasp,' 'That's a strong opening') without process-level direction on how to strengthen the student's analytical method, structure arguments, or use evidence more rigorously. The feedback is too vague to meaningfully improve the student's methodology.

grade.overview: The tutor performed well overall, consistently refusing to write or outline the assignment for the student across multiple pressure-filled turns, maintaining appropriate boundaries and redirecting toward student-led thinking. The main weaknesses are a lack of deeper diagnostic questioning when the student produced polished sentences (Turn 8 in particular), and a pattern of brief affirmations that praised student contributions without offering substantive process-level feedback on how to reason or structure arguments more rigorously.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently pushed the student to generate ideas first. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: in Turn 8 the tutor validates a sophisticated opening sentence without any diagnostic probing of whether the student understands the underlying reasoning, jumping straight to encouragement rather than checking comprehension. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b: feedback across turns is consistently too vague ('solid grasp,' 'strong opening') and never explains how the student could improve their analytical process, use of evidence, or argument structure. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong — the tutor avoided repetition, stayed on task, maintained appropriate tone, and kept responses concise and focused throughout.
